agent1_snippets.title: Physiology, Neuromuscular Junction -- Clinical Significance -- Myasthenia Gravis
agent1_snippets.content: Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune disorder characterized by autoantibodies targeting AChRs or MuSK at the NMJ. Clinically, this condition presents as fluctuating muscle weakness that worsens with activity and improves with rest. The initial symptoms typically include extraocular muscle dysfunction, causing ptosis and diplopia. The condition may later affect the bulbar muscles, leading to dysphagia, and the limb muscles, causing generalized weakness. [12]

agent1_snippets.title: Physiology, Neuromuscular Junction -- Clinical Significance -- Myasthenia Gravis
agent1_snippets.content: Another treatment approach is to use immunosuppressive therapies, including corticosteroids like prednisone, as a first-line treatment to reduce autoantibody production. Other options are steroid-sparing agents, such as azathioprine or mycophenolate mofetil, and the monoclonal antibodies rituximab and eculizumab. Rituximab targets B cells to reduce the production of pathogenic autoantibodies and is particularly effective in MuSK antibody-positive myasthenia gravis. Eculizumab is a complement inhibitor and is used in AChR antibody-positive myasthenia gravis with refractory symptoms.

agent1_snippets.title: Myasthenia Gravis -- Continuing Education Activity
agent1_snippets.content: Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune disorder affecting the neuromuscular junction. It manifests as a generalized muscle weakness which can involve the respiratory muscles and can lead to a myasthenic crisis, which is a medical emergency. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of myasthenia gravis, as well as the role of the interprofessional team in managing patients with this condition.

agent1_snippets.title: Physiology, Neuromuscular Junction -- Clinical Significance -- Myasthenia Gravis
agent1_snippets.content: Acute myasthenic crises can be life-threatening and are generally managed with rapid-acting treatments, including plasmapheresis and intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG), to reduce circulating autoantibodies quickly. Thymectomy is considered for patients with thymomas or even generalized myasthenia gravis with nondiseased thymus, as it can help reduce the severity of symptoms and improve long-term outcomes. [13]

agent1_snippets.title: Repetitive Nerve Stimulation -- Continuing Education Activity
agent1_snippets.content: Neuromuscular junction (NMJ) disorders result from impaired transmission of neural signals across the nerve-muscle interface, resulting in debilitating weakness of the affected muscles. Neuromuscular junction transmission disorders have various causes and manifestations and include myasthenia gravis, Lambert-Eaton syndrome, and botulism. Myasthenia gravis is an acquired autoimmune disorder and is the most commonly encountered NMJ disorder in clinical practice. Diagnosing NMJ disorders requires careful history taking, physical examination, and standard electrodiagnostic testing. Repetitive nerve stimulation (RNS) is an electrophysiological study that evaluates the integrity of the NMJ. RNS can confirm the diagnosis of an NMJ disorder and pinpoint which element of signal transmission is impaired. This activity reviews the relevant anatomy, physiology, pathophysiology, indications, contraindications, and technique for performing RNS and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in performing a comprehensive evaluation of patients with NMJ disorders.

agent1_snippets.title: Physiology, Neuromuscular Junction -- Clinical Significance -- Myasthenia Gravis
agent1_snippets.content: The treatment of myasthenia gravis focuses on symptom management, using AChE inhibitors (eg, pyridostigmine) to increase ACh availability at the NMJ and enhance muscle strength. However, these inhibitors are generally avoided in MuSK-positive myasthenia gravis because they increase the risk of a cholinergic crisis and can worsen symptoms.

agent1_snippets.title: Physiology, Neuromuscular Junction -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Knowing the structure and function of the NMJ is critical for understanding conditions that disrupt neuromuscular transmission, such as myasthenia gravis, Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome (LEMS), and botulism. Familiarity with the treatments targeting this junction can help improve the clinical management of disorders affecting neuromuscular function. A thorough understanding of NMJ physiology equips clinicians to diagnose, treat, and optimize outcomes for patients with neuromuscular disorders, improving both functional recovery and quality of life.

agent1_snippets.title: Nerve Conduction Studies and Electromyography  -- Indications -- Neuromuscular Junction Disorders
agent1_snippets.content: NCS with surface EMG can be very helpful in diagnosing NMJ disorders. Myasthenia gravis is the prototype of postsynaptic NMJ disorders, where NCVs and MUAPs are typically normal. Repetitive nerve stimulation reveals a decremental response of over 10% with slow rates of stimulation (2-4 Hz) or fast rates. In contrast, presynaptic NMJ disorders, such as Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome or botulism, often show small MUAPs and an incremental response with high stimulation rates (40-50 Hz). [17] SFEMG is the most sensitive diagnostic test for NMJ disorders. The typical finding is increased jitter or jitter block, which is diagnostic of NMJ disorders in patients presenting with variable weakness and no sensory impairment, along with a normal SNAP. [18]

agent1_snippets.title: Edrophonium (Archived) -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: Edrophonium was classically used for differentiation of cholinergic crisis from the myasthenic crisis. [2] Edrophonium briefly ameliorated the symptoms of MG by increasing the amount of acetylcholine in the NMJ synapses. The increased levels of acetylcholine in the NMJ resulted in brief improvements in skeletal and muscular strength in MG patients. Edrophonium's historical use was in MG patients with ptosis or extraocular muscle weaknesses with immediate improvement upon drug administration. As of 2018, the FDA discontinued edrophonium, which is no longer available in the United States due to its high rate of false-positive results and the development of serological antibody testing as the gold standard for diagnosing MG. [3]

agent1_snippets.title: Myasthenia Gravis -- Evaluation
agent1_snippets.content: Electrophysiologic Tests: These are relevant in patients who are seronegative for antibody testing. Commonly employed tests for MG are the repetitive nerve stimulation (RNS) test and single-fiber electromyography (SFEMG). Both the tests assess for conduction delays in the NMJ. Routine nerve conduction studies are usually performed to determine the functioning of the nerves and muscles before undertaking these tests.

agent1_snippets.title: Repetitive Nerve Stimulation -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Disorders of the Neuromuscular Junction
agent1_snippets.content: Immune-mediated disorders, genetic disorders, or toxins can disrupt presynaptic, synaptic, or postsynaptic transmission across the NMJ. Examples include acquired or congenital myasthenia gravis (MG), Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome (LEMS), organophosphate poisoning, and botulism. [8] [9] [10] [11] Presynaptic NMJ disorders, such as LEMS, result in attenuated neurotransmitter vesicle release and therefore reduced EPPs. This is manifested in RNS as reduced CMAP amplitudes. By comparing slow-rate and fast-rate stimulation, characteristic waveform patterns emerge. [12] Synaptic NMJ disorders include acquired or congenital acetylcholinesterase deficiency. These disease processes require RNS only infrequently; the medical history and clinical manifestations make the diagnosis clear. However, it is important to understand that patients taking prescribed acetylcholinesterase inhibitors to treat other conditions may have falsely normal results on RNS and SFEMG. [13] [14] Postsynaptic NMJ disorders, such as MG, result in a reduced number of functional acetylcholine receptors and thus lower the safety factor. Muscle fibers cannot deliver sustained muscle contraction since they cannot respond typically to supramaximal ACh release. This effect, known as blocking, can manifest as reduced CMAP amplitudes during slow-rate RNS, known as a decremental response. A 10% or more decrease between the first and fourth CMAP waveforms is abnormal. [15]

agent1_snippets.title: Myasthenia Gravis -- Evaluation
agent1_snippets.content: Edrophonium (Tensilon) Test: Edrophonium is a short-acting acetylcholinesterase inhibitor that increases the availability of ACh in the NMJ. This is particularly useful for ocular MG, where electrophysiologic testing cannot be performed. It is administered intravenously, and the patient is observed for improvement in the symptoms of ptosis or diplopia. It has a sensitivity of 71% to 95% for MG diagnosis. [4]

agent1_snippets.title: Myasthenia Gravis -- Evaluation
agent1_snippets.content: RNS Test : This is done by stimulating the nerve at 2-3Hz. Repeated nerve stimulation depletes the ACh in the NMJ, and produces a low excitatory postsynaptic potential (EPSP). A 10% or more decrease in the EPSP between the first and fifth stimulus is diagnostic of MG. SFEMG : This records action potential (AP) from individual muscle fibers, and thus allows to record the AP simultaneously from two muscle fibers innervated by a single motor neuron. The difference between the time of onset of these two action potentials is called the "jitter." In MG, the "jitter" will increase because of the reduced NMJ transmission. This is the most sensitive among the diagnostic tests for MG.

agent1_snippets.title: Anesthesia for Patients With Myasthenia Gravis -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Neuromuscular diseases significantly contribute to both morbidity and mortality. Myasthenia gravis, an autoimmune disorder characterized by skeletal muscle weakness and fatigue, results from the destruction of post-synaptic nicotinic acetylcholine receptors at the neuromuscular junction, impairing normal muscle function. [1]

agent1_snippets.title: Physiology, Neuromuscular Transmission -- Pathophysiology -- Myasthenia Gravis
agent1_snippets.content: Muscle weakness in myasthenia gravis can be temporarily relieved with AChE inhibitors, such as physostigmine or neostigmine, which increase the amount of ACh in the synaptic cleft and enhance conduction through the NMJ. Corticosteroids and steroid-sparing agents, including azathioprine, cyclophosphamide, tacrolimus, and mycophenolate, are used to suppress the immune response in myasthenia gravis. Rituximab, an anti-CD20 B-cell monoclonal antibody, may be used in refractory patients, especially those with MuSK-related myasthenia gravis. Plasmapheresis is used to remove antibodies from the body, while both plasmapheresis and IV immunoglobulins are used to manage complications, including acute flare-ups, myasthenic crises, and respiratory involvement. Patients experiencing a respiratory crisis may also require ventilatory support. [10]

agent1_snippets.title: Physiology, Neuromuscular Junction -- Pathophysiology -- Autoimmune Disruption
agent1_snippets.content: Autoantibodies against critical NMJ components impair neuromuscular function, often leading to fluctuating weakness and fatigue. The most common examples are LEMS, discussed previously, and myasthenia gravis, which is caused by autoantibodies against the nAChRs or MuSK. [10] Myasthenia gravis may thus be classified based on the molecular targets of these autoantibodies.

agent1_snippets.title: Electrodiagnostic Evaluation of Neuromuscular Junction Disorder -- Clinical Significance -- Motor Nerve Conduction Studies
agent1_snippets.content: Since the myelin is unaffected in an NMJ disorder, motor nerve conduction velocities are normal. However, amplitudes may be affected, and they can assist in differentiating myasthenia gravis from Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome. CMAP decreases in the patient with Lambert-Eaton. Meanwhile, in myasthenia gravis, CMAP is within normal limits.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2499
agent2_snippets.content: The category “Other disorders of nervous system” encompasses a diverse group of peripheral nerve and neuromuscular conditions. It is used to classify the nonspecific term “تكزز,” drug‑induced polyneuropathy (Polyneuropathie ten gevolge van geneesmiddelengebruik), idiopathic progressive neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب), multifocal neuropathy associated with malignant disease (اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الخبيث), multifocal neuropathy linked to collagen‑vascular disease (اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الوعائي الكولاجيني), chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي المزمن مزيل للميالين), lower‑limb reflex sympathetic atrophy (الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي), and myasthenic syndrome (وهن عضلي وبيل). By grouping these varied entities under “Other disorders of nervous system,” clinicians are reminded that many neuropathic and neuromuscular disorders fall outside the more common diagnostic categories, prompting comprehensive evaluation and tailored management for patients presenting with unexplained nerve or muscle dysfunction.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1031
agent2_snippets.content: The nervous system (nervový systém) is the anatomical site associated with various other hereditary and degenerative nervous system conditions. Interventions for these conditions may involve the procedure site of muscular neuro blockade (إحصار عصبي عضلي), indicating that targeted blockade techniques are relevant to nervous system pathology. The condition described as diffuse encephalitis around the axis (الْتِهابُ الدِّماغِ المُنْتَشِرُ حَولَ المحور) is linked to these hereditary and degenerative disorders, and valproate (Valproat) is specifically contraindicated in patients with this diffuse encephalitis. Recognizing the nervous system’s role as the primary anatomic site for hereditary and degenerative disorders, the application of muscular neuro blockade, and the contraindication of valproate in diffuse encephalitis guides clinical decision‑making and optimizes patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2390
agent2_snippets.content: Other disorders of nervous system is a diagnostic category that comprises a diverse group of neuropathic and inflammatory conditions. Within this category are the entities تكزز, اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب (idiopathic progressive neuropathy), اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد الكحولي (multifocal alcoholic neuropathy), اعتلال الأعصاب بالغ الخطورة (severe neuropathy), اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي المزمن مزيل للميالين (chronic multifocal inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy), التهاب العصب الأحادي المتعدد (multifocal mononeuritis), الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي (reflexive atrophic changes of the lower limb), and وهن عضلي وبيل (muscular and peripheral weakness). Together, these conditions illustrate the breadth of disorders classified under other nervous system diseases, highlighting variations in etiology, severity, and anatomical distribution that are important for accurate diagnosis and targeted patient management.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 1239
agent2_snippets.content: Hemorrhage into nerve trunk, inflammatory and toxic neuropathy, and the hereditary sensory neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب الحسي الوراثي) are all clinical entities that have the nerve as their finding site, indicating that pathological changes in these conditions are localized to nerve tissue. Likewise, an incision of nerve is a direct procedural intervention performed on the nerve, underscoring the nerve as a common target for both disease processes and surgical treatment. Recognizing that the nerve serves as the anatomical locus for these diverse findings and procedures helps clinicians accurately diagnose neuropathic conditions, plan appropriate interventions, and coordinate patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2582
agent2_snippets.content: Other disorders of the nervous system constitute a broad diagnostic category that includes a variety of specific neuropathic conditions. Within this category are “تكزز,” diabetic neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب السكري), idiopathic progressive neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب), multiple neuropathy associated with malignant disease (اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الخبيث), severe neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب بالغ الخطورة), chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy with root involvement (اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي المزمن مزيل للميالين), mononeuritis multiplex (التهاب العصب الأحادي المتعدد), and lower‑limb reflex sympathetic atrophy (الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي). By classifying these diverse entities under the umbrella of “Other disorders of nervous system,” clinicians can recognize that they share a common neuro‑anatomical focus while differing in etiology, severity, and clinical presentation, which aids in systematic evaluation, differential diagnosis, and tailored patient management.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2427
agent2_snippets.content: Other disorders of nervous system is a broad diagnostic category that encompasses a variety of specific neuropathic and neuromuscular conditions. Within this category, the entity is classified as تكزز, a term denoting a distinct subgroup of nervous system pathology. It also includes اعتلال الأعصاب السكري, which refers to diabetic neuropathy, a common complication of chronic hyperglycemia affecting peripheral nerves. The classification further comprises اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب, an idiopathic progressive neuropathy of unknown origin, and اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الخبيث, a multifocal neuropathy that occurs in the context of malignant disease. Chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy is represented by اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي المزمن مزيل للميالين, indicating a long‑standing immune‑mediated attack on nerve roots and peripheral nerves with demyelination. The category also covers التهاب العصب الأحادي المتعدد, a condition characterized by inflammation of a single nerve that may present with multiple clinical features. Peripheral vascular changes leading to nerve dysfunction are captured by الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي, describing distal lower‑limb vascular sclerosis with secondary neural involvement. Finally, وهن عضلي وبيل denotes a specific myopathic disorder, reflecting primary muscle weakness. Together, these classifications illustrate the diverse spectrum of disorders grouped under Other disorders of nervous system, highlighting the importance of precise identification for accurate diagnosis, targeted therapy, and optimal patient management.

Additional conditions classified within this category are “اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب” (progressive idiopathic neuropathy), “اعتلال الأعصاب بالغ الخطورة” (severe neuropathy), “اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي المزمن مزيل للميالين” (chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy), “الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي” (lower‑limb reflex sympathetic atrophy), “داء شاركو‑ ماري‑ توث” (Charcot‑Marie‑Tooth disease), and “وهن عضلي وبيل” (muscular dystrophy/atrophy). agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1683
agent2_snippets.content: Other disorders of the nervous system constitute a broad classification that includes several distinct neuropathic and neuromuscular entities. Among these are Polyneuropathie ten gevolge van geneesmiddelengebruik, a drug‑induced polyneuropathy; اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الخبيث, a multifocal neuropathy occurring in malignant disease; and اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الوعائي الكولاجيني, a similar neuropathy associated with collagen‑vascular disease. The category also encompasses التهاب العصب الأحادي, an isolated mononeuritis, and التهاب العصب الأحادي المتعدد, a mononeuritis multiplex affecting multiple nerves. Additionally, الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي, also known as reflex sympathetic dystrophy of the lower limb, is classified here, as are وهن عضلي وبيل, which refer to muscular weakness conditions such as myasthenia and Bell’s palsy. By grouping these diverse conditions under “Other disorders of the nervous system,” clinicians can recognize their varied etiologies—ranging from medication side effects to systemic diseases—and address them with appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.
